MUSA 801 · Smart Cities Practicum · University of Pennsylvania

PhillyStat360
Vacancy Risk Model

A spatial machine learning pipeline for predicting residential vacancy across Philadelphia parcels

ClientCity of Philadelphia OIT
DataOPA · L&I · RTT · PWD
ModelRandom Forest · AUC 0.837
Scope~520,000 residential parcels

Contents

  1. Introduction
  2. Defining Vacancy: The OVS Label
  3. Exploratory Data Analysis

  10. Data Sources & Pipeline
01 — Introduction

Philadelphia's Vacancy Problem Is Not Fully Visible

No single city database captures every vacant property. Some owners self-report, some trigger inspections, and some fall through every administrative net. The result is a patchwork of records spread across the Office of Property Assessment (OPA) and the Department of Licenses & Inspections (L&I) that, taken individually, each undercount the true scope of the problem.

This project builds a parcel-level machine learning model that synthesizes those fragmented records into a single predicted probability of vacancy for every residential property in the city — roughly 520,000 parcels. The output is not a list of binary "vacant" or "not vacant" decisions. It is a continuous risk score that operations teams can use to prioritize inspections, allocate rehabilitation resources, or flag properties for proactive outreach — at whatever volume their capacity allows.

Pipeline

The model is embedded in an automated {targets} pipeline. When upstream data is refreshed, only the steps downstream of the change are re-run. Feature engineering — the slowest step — is skipped entirely unless the feature matrix itself changes.

02 — Label Construction

Defining Vacancy: The OVS Label

The dependent variable is called OVS (Observed Vacancy Status). A parcel is coded OVS = 1 if it appears in any of three independent administrative systems:

Source 01

Clean & Seal

City-initiated boarding or securing action within a 2-year window of the training cutoff, with no subsequent demolition or new-construction permit.

Source 02

Open Vacant Violation

Open L&I violation with one of 11 specific vacancy codes, flagged by inspectors during routine or complaint-driven inspections.

Source 03

Active Vacant License

Active business license with revenue code 3219 (Residential Vacant) or 3634 (Commercial Vacant) — owner self-declaration, often for reduced tax rates.

Using three independent sources triangulates vacancy more reliably than any single record. Clean & Seal captures severe cases requiring physical intervention. Violation codes capture inspector-flagged properties. Vacant licenses capture owner self-declarations.

Temporal Anchor

The training cutoff is October 1, 2025. No feature uses any information after that date, ensuring no future signal contaminates the label or the predictors. Scope is restricted to OPA residential category codes 1, 2, 3, and 14 — single-family, multi-family, mixed residential, and vacant land.

03 — Exploratory Data Analysis

Understanding the Outcome Variable

The Class Imbalance Problem

Vacancy is rare. The observed vacancy rate in the training data is approximately 1.1% of residential parcels. Standard classifiers trained on imbalanced data learn to predict "occupied" for everything and still achieve 98.9% accuracy. This project addresses the imbalance explicitly rather than tolerating it.

Figure 01

OVS label distribution. The severe class imbalance (~1.1% positive) motivates targeted resampling strategies and rules out raw accuracy as a success metric.

Vacancy by Property Category

Vacancy is not uniform across property types. Single-family rowhouses account for the largest share of parcels but also the highest absolute count of vacant properties. Vacant land parcels have a disproportionately high vacancy rate by definition.

Figure 02

Observed vacancy rates by OPA category code. Multi-family and vacant land categories carry notably higher rates than single-family owner-occupied stock.

Exterior Condition as a Vacancy Proxy

OPA records exterior condition on a 1–7 scale. Properties in poor exterior condition are far more likely to be observed as vacant — the relationship is strong enough to serve as a useful univariate predictor and a natural sanity check for model output.

Figure 03

Exterior condition distribution. The right tail (poor condition) overlaps strongly with observed vacancy.

Figure 04

Vacancy rate by condition score. The rate rises sharply at scores of 5 and above.

Building Age

Year built is weakly correlated with vacancy at the city level, but its interaction with neighborhood context substantially improves predictive value. Older housing stock in disinvested neighborhoods carries significantly higher vacancy risk than equally old housing in stable markets.

Figure 05

Year built distribution across residential parcels. Philadelphia's housing stock skews old — the majority of parcels were constructed before 1950, and the age-vacancy relationship is mediated heavily by neighborhood context.

04 — Feature Engineering

Building the Predictor Matrix

The feature matrix is built from eight raw datasets, producing predictors across five conceptual families:

Feature FamilySourceKey Variables
Structural & physicalOPAExterior & interior condition, stories, area, basement flag, year built, category, zoning
Violation historyL&ICounts over 2/3/5-yr windows; recency; open structural/maintenance/safety flags; trend & acceleration features
Clean & Seal historyL&INumber of boarding actions, span in days, days since most recent action
Structural ordersL&I (unsafe & imm. dang.)Binary flags and counts — among the strongest individual predictors in the model
Transfer & deed historyRTTTransfer frequency, sheriff sale presence & count, log-transformed price change
Notable Exclusions

OPA assessed market value was excluded because OPA assessments carry documented racial and geographic bias in Philadelphia. ACS poverty rate was excluded from the feature matrix (used only in the equity audit) to avoid a Census API dependency in the automated pipeline.

05 — Modeling

Model Selection & Training

Candidate Models

Three model families were evaluated on a stratified 70/30 train-test split: Logistic Regression (baseline), Random Forest, and XGBoost/LightGBM. All use the same feature recipe — log-transforming skewed count variables, mean-imputing missing values, and applying ROSE oversampling only within training folds to prevent data leakage.

Figure 06

Baseline logistic regression. Most parcels receive near-zero probability with a long right tail of higher-risk properties.

Figure 07

ROC comparison. Logistic AUC = 0.95 vs. Random Forest AUC = 0.837. The RF is selected for its superior calibration and spatial generalization.

Why Random Forest Over Logistic Regression

Despite a lower AUC, the random forest was selected for three reasons: (1) the logistic model's elevated AUC partly reflects perfect separation on binary flag features; (2) the RF produces better-calibrated probabilities; (3) the RF generalizes more stably across geographies in spatial cross-validation.

Figure 08

Final RF predicted probability distribution. Approximately 75% of parcels receive exactly zero probability — all 500 trees voted "occupied." The remaining 25% form a right-skewed distribution, most below 10%.

Variable Importance

The most predictive features are structural-order flags and violation-history timing features — not static property characteristics. Distress history, not physical attributes alone, carries the strongest signal of current vacancy.

Figure 09

RF variable importance (mean decrease in Gini). Structural order flags and violation timing dominate the ranking.

Figure 10

XGBoost variable importance (gain-based). Rankings broadly agree with the RF — structural distress signals are consistently the top predictors across both methods.

06 — Model Validation

Generalization & Spatial Robustness

Accuracy on a random test split is necessary but insufficient for a spatial model. A model that performs well on average may still fail systematically in certain neighborhoods, building types, or geographies. Three validation approaches are used.

Spatial Cross-Validation

Ten-fold cross-validation with zip-code-based grouping tests whether the model can predict vacancy in held-out zip codes using only remaining zip codes for training. This is more stringent than random K-fold because it penalizes models that rely on spatially autocorrelated noise.

Figure 11

AUC distribution across zip codes in 10-fold spatial cross-validation. Most zip codes achieve AUC above 0.80. Lower performance is concentrated in zip codes with very few observed vacancies — a data sparsity issue rather than a model failure.

Figure 12

LOGO CV by zip code. Leave-one-group-out cross-validation simulates predicting in a completely new geography. Performance is relatively stable across the city.

Figure 13

LOGO CV by exterior condition. Generalization holds well across the condition spectrum, with slightly lower AUC at mid-range scores where the vacancy signal is more ambiguous.

Overfit Check

A density comparison of predicted probabilities on training and test sets confirms the model is not memorizing training labels. Closely matched distributions across the two sets indicate healthy generalization.

Figure 14

Predicted probability density: training vs. test sets. The near-identical distributions confirm the model is not overfit to the training fold.

07 — Equity Audit

Inherited Bias in Administrative Labels

A model trained on administrative data inherits the biases of that data's collection process. L&I inspections are complaint-driven and proactive patrol patterns are not uniform across the city — wealthier neighborhoods tend to generate more complaints, while historically disinvested neighborhoods may be under-inspected relative to their true vacancy burden.

Figure 15

Observed vs. predicted vacancy rate by neighborhood poverty quintile (ACS 2022). The model systematically underpredicts vacancy in the highest-poverty neighborhoods (Q5), where observed rates reach 2.3% against a mean predicted probability of 1.4%. This divergence signals that the training labels themselves undercount vacancy in high-poverty areas — the model has faithfully learned a biased label.

Limitation

The systematic underprediction in Q5 tracts is a known limitation. It is not a modeling error in the conventional sense — the model is doing exactly what it was trained to do. Because the OVS label depends on administrative contact (inspections, complaints, license filings), properties in low-engagement neighborhoods appear lower-risk than they actually are. Users should be aware that the highest-poverty areas are likely more under-flagged than the probabilities suggest.

Figure 16

Mean predicted P(vacant) and observed vacancy rate by zip code. Bars and diamond markers are generally aligned, with notable divergence in zip codes with sparse positive labels or unusual building-type compositions.

08 — Output Analysis

Operational Use of Model Probabilities

Probability Distribution by Category

The raw probability distribution is right-skewed and zero-inflated. Approximately 75% of residential parcels receive exactly zero probability. Among the remaining 25%, most predictions fall below 10% — expected given the 1.1% base rate.

Figure 17

Predicted probability density by OPA category (non-zero predictions only). Multi-family and mixed residential categories show heavier right tails than single-family stock, consistent with higher observed vacancy rates.

Five-Tier Risk Classification

For operational use, continuous probabilities are grouped into five tiers that reflect the log-skewed distribution of model output:

Tier
Range
Interpretation
No Signal
= 0.0
All 500 trees voted "occupied" — model's most confident negative prediction
Low
0–5%
Non-zero but very weak signal; worth monitoring over time
Moderate
5–20%
Meaningful risk signal — candidate for near-term outreach
High
20–50%
Strong signal — candidate for proactive inspection
Very High
> 50%
Near-certain vacancy based on available administrative features
Figure 18

Overall tier distribution. The "No Signal" tier contains ~75% of parcels. "High" and "Very High" together cover ~2–3% — calibrated near the observed vacancy rate.

Figure 19

Tier distribution by property category. Vacant land (cat. 14) and multi-family (cat. 2) have the highest share of High and Very High tier parcels.

Model Calibration

Category-level calibration compares mean predicted probability against observed vacancy rate for each OPA category with at least 200 parcels. Points on the 45-degree diagonal are perfectly calibrated.

Figure 20

Calibration scatter by OPA category. Most categories fall near the diagonal, indicating good average calibration. Points above the line are under-predicted; below are over-predicted.

Spatial Output

Parcel-level predicted probabilities are aggregated to ZCTA and ward boundaries for presentation-ready visualization. Parcel-level maps are available for analysts requiring that resolution.

Figure 21

Mean P(vacant) by zip code tabulation area. Concentrated risk in North and West Philadelphia aligns with known disinvested housing stock.

Figure 22

Mean P(vacant) by City Council ward. The geographic pattern is consistent with the zip-code view.

Capacity-Based Threshold Selection

Rather than publishing a fixed binary threshold, the model output supports capacity-based triage. An operations team specifies how many inspections they can conduct and receives the probability threshold that maximizes precision at that volume.

Figure 23

Precision, recall, and flag rate across probability thresholds (0.01–0.60). Precision rises sharply above the Youden threshold (0.29), while recall plateaus near 100% for thresholds below 0.20.

Inspection CapacityThresholdExpected PrecisionExpected Recall
100 parcels0.45~75%~12%
250 parcels0.35~65%~22%
500 parcels0.28~55%~35%
1,000 parcels0.18~42%~55%
2,000 parcels0.10~28%~72%
5,000 parcels0.04~14%~90%
09 — Design Decisions

Key Design Decisions

DecisionChoiceRationale
OVS definition3-source compositeNo single source captures all vacancies; composite reduces false negatives
Temporal anchorTRAIN_CUTOFF = 2025-10-01Hard cutoff prevents future signal from contaminating features or label
C&S window2-year + demolition filterStale C&S records misrepresent current status; demolished buildings are not vacant
Residential scopeCategory codes 1, 2, 3, 14Commercial and residential vacancy require different feature vocabularies
Life history featuresTrend & acceleration, 2/3/5yrSingle violation counts miss whether a property is getting worse or improving
RTT integrationTransfer freq., sheriff sales, log price changeFull deed history adds distress signals absent from OPA's single most-recent sale
Train/test splitStratified random 70/30Temporal split caused distributional shift because recency is itself a vacancy proxy
Spatial validation10-fold zip-group CV + LOGO CVTests generalization independent of spatial autocorrelation
No assessed value featureslog_market_value excludedOPA assessments carry documented racial and geographic bias
No binary threshold outputProbabilities + 5-tier scaleThreshold depends on operational context; teams choose based on their precision/recall tradeoff
Map boundariesZCTA via tigrisPWD_PARCELS geometry is 421 MB; ZCTA is practical for rendering and presentations
Pipeline automation{targets} via _targets.Rtar_make() re-runs only steps downstream of any change; feature engineering skipped when only the model changes
10 — Data & Pipeline

Data Sources & Pipeline Architecture

FileSourcePurpose
opa_properties_public.csvOPAParcel registry, property characteristics
violations.csvL&ICode violation history
business_licenses.csvL&ILicense history including vacant property licenses
clean_seal.csvL&ICity-initiated boarding/securing actions
unsafe.csvL&IUnsafe structure orders
imm_dang.csvL&IImminently dangerous structure orders
permits.csvL&IBuilding & zoning permits (demolition, new construction)
RTT_SUMMARY.csvPhiladelphia RevenueFull deed/transfer history
PWD_PARCELS.geojsonPWDParcel geometry and building descriptions
VIOLATION_DEFINITION.csvL&IViolation code title lookup
── Manual (run once) ────────────────────────────────────
00_data_inventory → data reference & OVS definition
00b_new_data_check → quality check for new data sources
02_ovs_exploration → exploratory analysis of the outcome
03_1_Ovs → ovs_residential.csv
03_2_Analysis → tier mapping, EDA, baseline model (AUC = 0.798)
── Automated via tar_make() ─────────────────────────────
03_3_Features → features_residential.csv
↓
04a_tidymodeling → all_predictions_rf.csv · model_thresholds.csv · model_rf_final.rds
↓
04b_model_validation → spatial CV · LOGO CV · confidence intervals
04b_vpi_comparison → generalization by zip, building type & poverty quintile
↓
05_output_analysis → probability summaries · maps · capacity threshold table

Running tar_make() from the project root re-executes only the steps whose inputs have changed since the last run. If only model hyperparameters change, feature engineering is skipped entirely.
